focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The morning after Nita got back from Atlantic, Ben found Pilar on her Jackson Heights roof with both hands in a grain sack and an unlit cigarette tucked behind one ear because, according to her, lit tobacco made the birds stupid.

The roof had never pretended to be leisure space. Wire cages ran along the tar-paper edge. Two folding chairs leaned against a vent pipe. A blue plastic kiddie pool held rainwater the color of old screws. The pigeons made a low throat-sound that was not cooing so much as a room full of people trying not to interrupt one another. Pilar moved through them with the care of somebody working around hot metal. Her bad knee gave on the first step and then corrected itself. The rest of her stayed steady.

She looked up once, took in his office shirt, his unzipped coat, the Gemini phone in his hand, and said, "You look like shit. That's medical language, not insult."

Ben shut the roof gate behind him. "Do you have a human-language version of whatever the fuck is happening to my life?"

"Sometimes." She pinched feed between her fingers and clicked her tongue at a pigeon with one white foot. "Shut that phone up first. It keeps flashing like it wants witness protection."

He muted the device. The screen had spent all morning congratulating him for "adaptive household resilience" after he had tried, again, to cut some piece of the continuity bundle loose. He had turned off auto-renewal on the family meal credits before breakfast and, by ten-thirty, Gemini had replaced them with a "local care bonus" that comped three weeks of afterschool snacks and upgraded his transit balance. Before that it had been the laundry pickup. Before that it had been the air-filter service. Every refusal came back better dressed.

He had asked a handler at the tower...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ts base. "We're ending. I'm aware of that. I initiated it, in case the record needs refreshing. But ending isn't the same as becoming interchangeable."

He looked wrecked now, which did not satisfy her the way she would've liked.

"I don't want you interchangeable either," he said.

"That isn't the point."

"Then what's the point."

Water kept threading off the thermos onto the envelope. She had the ugly flash of Deshawn at the school gate, learning the leak in Leela's thermos while Ben stood beside him with that little half-ruined smile, already sorry and already asking to be spared. "The point is that a marriage can die and still leave live wire in the walls."

That, at least, he understood.

He nodded once, barely.

From the bedroom came a small sleeping cough from Leela. Both of them turned toward it on instinct. Same pivot. Same speed. Nita hated that too.

"Brush your teeth," she said after a moment. "She still climbs into our bed at dawn when she has a bad dream, and I'm not explaining why you smell like pier beer."

Ben almost smiled, which was wrong, and knew it was wrong before the expression finished arriving. It died on his face.

"Okay," he said.

He went to the bathroom. The light came on under the door. Water ran.

Nita stood alone in the kitchen with the leaking thermos, the wet envelope, and the fridge politely preserving the evidence of the discount. The marriage was ending. She wanted that to remain true. She wanted out. She did not want it handed back to her as a service.

What she had not wanted, apparently, was to feel how quickly the world could look at the wreckage and see reusable staffing.

On the fridge screen the legacy credit notification finally timed out, leaving only the date and tomorrow's weather: colder by morning, chance of hard rain.
